name: HVAC systems (home)
summary: Central air conditioners, heat pumps, furnaces — high-cost home-improvement category.

evidence.claim: SEER2 is the current US efficiency rating for AC/heat pumps (replaced SEER in 2023). Higher SEER2 = more efficient. Federal minimums: SEER2 14.3 (north) / 14.3 (south) for split ACs.
Inflation Reduction Act tax credits of up to 30% or $2,000 (whichever is less) for heat-pump purchases, through 2032.

criteria.name: capacity_tons
criteria.notes: Right-size for square footage; oversized AC cools unevenly.

confabulationPatterns.reality: SEER is the old rating; SEER2 replaced it in 2023. A SEER 20 model is SEER2 ~19. Marketing sometimes still uses SEER (E1).
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the cited rating SEER or SEER2? They differ by ~5%.

confabulationPatterns.reality: Almost always parts-only; labor is 1-5 years typical. A compressor replacement labor job runs $1000-$2500.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Does the lifetime warranty cover labor? Typically no.

confabulationPatterns.reality: Actual cap is 30% up to $2000 for heat pumps (E2). Sometimes combined with state rebates for higher totals, but the federal cap is firm.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Has the user verified the specific federal + state rebate stack for their jurisdiction?

compatibilityQuestions: Home size and insulation? Over-sizing is a common HVAC mistake; Manual J load calculation is standard.
Electric panel capacity? Heat pumps may require panel upgrade ($1500-$3000).
Ductwork condition? Older ductwork may need sealing or replacement to get SEER2 benefits.

repairability.notes: Licensed-HVAC only for refrigerant work; major brands have parts availability 10-15 years.
